stories.original:
**UK King's Speech confirms merger of Payment Systems Regulator into FCA**

*The UK government has formally committed to folding the PSR into the Financial Conduct Authority, reshaping the supervisory architecture for payments.*

The legislative agenda set out in the King's Speech on May 11 includes a Financial Services Bill that will merge the Payment Systems Regulator into the FCA. The move, long discussed as part of the government's broader deregulatory push, will consolidate payments oversight under a single conduct authority. The Bill is also expected to reform the Financial Ombudsman Service.

For the payments industry, the merger raises immediate questions about how the FCA will absorb the PSR's specific mandates, particularly on interchange regulation, APP fraud reimbursement and access to payment systems. The PSR has been an assertive regulator in these areas, and the transition will test whether that posture survives integration into a larger body with competing priorities.

The announcement is a commitment to legislate, not a finished framework. Industry participants should watch for the Bill's detailed provisions on how existing PSR powers and ongoing enforcement actions will transfer.

*Editorial note: This is the most consequential UK regulatory development in the feed — a structural change to payments supervision that will affect every participant in UK payment systems.*

*Sources: [NHS England abolition; PSR merger into FCA; FOS reforms; cost of living in King's Speech](https://healthcareandprotection.com/nhs-england-abolition-psr-merger-into-fca-fos-reforms-cost-of-living-in-kings-speech-reports/) — Healthcare & Protection | [UK Government Unveils Legislative Agenda to Address Key Economic and Welfare Issues](https://news.ssbcrack.com/uk-government-unveils-legislative-agenda-to-address-key-economic-and-welfare-issues/) — SSBCrack | [King's Speech 2026 Unveils Major UK Reform Agenda](https://mabumbe.com/people/kings-speech-2026-unveils-major-uk-reform-agenda/) — Mabumbe*

stories.reason: not news, king's speech is just formal announcement

stories.original:
**AMLA flags instant payments and crypto as growing money laundering vectors**

*The EU's new anti-money laundering authority has identified instant payments among the channels where AML controls are failing to keep pace with risk.*

The Anti-Money Laundering Authority published a risk assessment warning that AI-enabled fraud, crypto-asset flows and the expansion of instant payments are widening gaps in Europe's AML defences. The report highlights that the speed and irrevocability of instant payments create particular challenges for transaction monitoring and intervention.

The timing matters. The EU's Instant Payments Regulation, which mandates SEPA instant payment capability across the bloc, is driving rapid adoption. AMLA's assessment is a signal that supervisors expect firms to invest in real-time fraud detection and sanctions screening capabilities that match the speed of the rails, not retrofit batch-era controls onto instant infrastructure.

*Editorial note: With instant payment mandates now in effect, AMLA's explicit identification of instant payments as a growing risk vector is a practical compliance signal for banks and PSPs across Europe.*

*Sources: [AMLA warns of widening AML gaps as fraud, crypto and sanctions risks intensify](https://ieu-monitoring.com/editorial/amla-warns-of-widening-aml-gaps-as-fraud-crypto-and-sanctions-risks-intensify/1214196?utm_source=ieu-portal) — IEU Monitoring*

stories.original:
**Admiral Money uses open banking data for AI-driven credit pricing**

*The partnership between Admiral Money and D•One illustrates how open banking data is moving from identity verification into core credit decisioning.*

Admiral Money, the lending arm of UK insurer Admiral Group, has partnered with D•One to deploy an AI-powered credit pricing model that draws on open banking transaction data. The model surfaces both positive and negative risk signals from customer banking records, enabling more granular risk segmentation than traditional credit bureau data alone.

This is part of a broader pattern in UK and European lending. Open banking started in credit primarily as a tool for income verification and affordability checks. Its use in dynamic pricing models, where real-time transaction data informs the rate a borrower is offered, represents a more commercially significant application. For lenders, the appeal is obvious: better risk discrimination means fewer defaults and more competitive pricing for lower-risk borrowers.

The deal also reflects the maturing supplier landscape. D•One's model sits on top of open banking infrastructure provided by others, suggesting the value chain is stratifying into data access, enrichment and decisioning layers.

*Editorial note: This story illustrates a concrete, commercially meaningful use of open banking data beyond payments, relevant to the open finance trajectory in Europe.*

*Sources: [Admiral Money partners with D•One for smarter credit pricing](https://fintech.global/2026/05/11/admiral-money-partners-with-done-for-smarter-credit-pricing/) — FinTech Global*

stories.original:
**Open finance positioned as a counter to AI-driven synthetic identity fraud**

*As synthetic identities become harder to detect with traditional methods, open finance data offers a verification route grounded in bank-held records.*

FinTech Magazine published an analysis arguing that open finance infrastructure offers a structural defence against synthetic identity fraud, one of the fastest-growing fraud types in financial services. The core proposition is straightforward: bank-held records are harder to fabricate than the credit file attributes that synthetic identities typically exploit, and open finance APIs provide a standardised way to verify against them.

The argument has weight in the European context, where the upcoming open finance framework under FiDA will expand the scope of data that can be shared with consent beyond payment accounts. If identity verification becomes a primary use case, it strengthens the commercial rationale for open finance beyond lending and account aggregation.

This is not a new idea, but the convergence of more sophisticated AI-generated fraud and wider regulatory support for data sharing is accelerating interest. For firms building fraud prevention stacks, the question is whether open finance verification can be embedded at onboarding speed without adding friction.

*Editorial note: Connects two major industry themes — AI-driven fraud and open finance — in a way that has direct implications for product and compliance teams.*

*Sources: [How Open Finance Combats Synthetic Fraud in the AI Era](https://fintechmagazine.com/news/how-open-finance-combats-synthetic-fraud-in-the-ai-era) — FinTech Magazine*

stories.original:
**UK government consults on commercial credit data sharing alongside open finance**

*A government response on SME access to finance policy signals that commercial credit data sharing will develop in tandem with the UK's open finance agenda.*

The UK government has published a response to its consultation on access to finance policy, covering commercial credit data sharing (CCDS) and bank referral schemes. Notably, the response indicates the government will take further evidence on what data categories to include and how to balance CCDS reporting with its broader open finance framework.

This matters because it confirms that UK policymakers see CCDS and open finance as complementary rather than competing channels. For SME lenders and credit reference agencies, the practical implication is that the data infrastructure for commercial lending is likely to be shaped by both mandated reporting obligations and consent-based open finance flows.

The consultation response stops short of setting a timetable, but its publication alongside the King's Speech legislative agenda suggests the Financial Services Bill may provide a vehicle for implementation.

*Editorial note: This is a policy signal with practical implications for the UK open finance framework's scope beyond retail banking, directly relevant to the SME lending and credit data ecosystem.*

*Sources: [Government response – Access to finance policy: commercial credit data sharing and bank referrals](https://www.regulationtomorrow.com/2026/05/government-response-access-to-finance-policy-commercial-credit-data-sharing-and-bank-referrals/) — Regulation Tomorrow*

---
